figures.caption: Figure 1: REEF-GP architecture. Gθ is a frozen neural operator that maps the input functions u, a to the output function v. REEF-GP models Gθ’s residual discrepancy by operating on the concatenation (C) of u,x,h in the functional regression form (F). Internally, it transforms (ρ) the learned geometryaware embeddings of Gθ’s internal layers (h1,hl,hL) to a new space where the kernel operates. In this example, the embedding in the feature-coordinate kernel space tears off the geometry exactly at the shock wave locations. The discrepancy δ is added to the base prediction after recovering (R) the point cloud form to obtain the output distribution.

Table 1: Evaluation metrics on representative 2D (Airfoil) and 3D (ShapeNet Car) benchmarks. Lower is better (↓). Best in bold, second best underlined. Base is the deterministic Transolver without UQ; rankings exclude Base. REEF-GP is our approach. Metrics are defined in Appendix H.

Figure 2: Predictive standard deviation fields on representative 2D (Airfoil) and 3D (ShapeNet Car) benchmarks. Top two rows show an airfoil sample and bottom two rows a car sample. For each sample the rows show in order: ground truth, base prediction, base error and the standard deviation of a sample for each of the UQ baselines.

Figure 4: Layer-wise CKA similarity across benchmarks. For each candidate Transolver layer Hl, we plot CKA similarity to the input layer H0 (solid) and to the output layer HL (dashed) as a function of depth. Intermediate layers are jointly distinct from both endpoints and our middle-layer selection chooses the maximally distinct one.

Table 2: Hyperparameter configurations for our method across benchmarks. Almost the same configuration is used for all five datasets with minimal per-task tuning, demonstrating robustness across varied geometries and physical regimes.

Figure 5: NLL loss curves across benchmarks for five seeds. Solid lines represent the mean NLL, with shaded regions indicating one standard deviation. To prevent overfitting of the GP hyperparameters, we continuously track the validation loss and employ early stopping. The optimal stopping point, where validation loss reaches its minimum, is marked by the dotted vertical line and point in each subplot.

Figure 6: Deformation flow of the geometry for an airfoil sample with shock waves above and below the airfoil. Each panel shows the two-dimensional t-SNE representations of the pointwise embeddings of the initial geometry at each of the 8 layers of the Transolver backbone. Across depth, the network progressively deforms the original geometry into a latent manifold, supporting the view that intermediate layers consistently encode geometry-aware structure.

Figure 7: Deformation flow of the geometry for an airfoil sample with a shock wave below the airfoil. Each panel shows the two-dimensional t-SNE representations of the pointwise embeddings of the initial geometry at each of the 8 layers of the Transolver backbone.

Figure 8: Feature map from Transolver embeddings to the GP kernel space. Top: airfoil sample with shock waves at both upper and lower surfaces. Bottom: airfoil sample with shock wave at the lower surface. The first three columns show t-SNE projections of the three CKA-selected Transolver layers used to construct the augmented state h(u,a,x); the rightmost column shows the embedding after the spectral-normalized projection ρ(·) used inside the kernel. The GP kernel space tears precisely at the shock wave region, indicating that the projection reshapes the operator’s latent geometry into a metric space where uncertainty can localize around physically meaningful error regions.

Table 3: Layer selection ablation. Effect of the layer set used to construct the augmented state h. Lower is better (↓). L0 uses only the input layer; Llast only the output; All layers concatenates the entire stack; REEF-GP selects {L0, l ∗, Llast} via CKA. Best per metric in bold, second best underlined.
Table 4: Training samples ablation on Airfoil. Effect of the number of training samples used to fit our GP discrepancy model. Lower is better (↓). Best per metric in bold, second best underlined.

Figure 9: Layer-selection ablation on Airfoil. Top: ground truth field, base prediction, absolute error. Bottom: predicted standard deviation under four layer-selection variants. All variants concentrate uncertainty around the shock front; the CKA-selected three-layer combination (REEF-GP) closely matches the full-stack baseline at reduced time and memory costs.

Figure 10: Training samples ablation on Airfoil. Each row corresponds to a different training-set size Mtrain, applied jointly to the base Transolver and the GP discrepancy model. Columns: ground truth field, base prediction, absolute error of the base prediction, and predicted standard deviation. As Mtrain decreases, the base operator’s error grows; REEF-GP correctly inflates its predicted uncertainty to track this growth, with the spatial structure of the predicted standard deviation closely matching the spatial structure of the error.
Table 5: Subset-size ablation on Airfoil. Effect of the per-expert support-set size Ns used at inference. For the results in text we use Ns = 5000. Lower is better (↓).

Figure 13: Number of gPoE experts ablation on Airfoil. Top: ground truth field, base prediction, absolute error. Bottom: predicted standard deviation under three values of K, the number of GP experts in the gPoE inference scheme.

Figure 14: Heteroscedastic vs. homoscedastic noise ablation on Airfoil. Top: ground truth, base prediction, absolute error. Bottom: predicted standard deviation under each noise model. The heteroscedastic model concentrates uncertainty around the shock front where the operator’s error is largest.
Table 8: Architecture and hyperparameter configurations. The base Transolver slightly varies across the benchmarks.
Table 9: Dataset splits. The number of samples slightly varies across the five benchmarks.

Table 10: Evaluation metrics for the three benchmarks not in the main text. Lower is better (↓). Best in bold, second best underlined. Rankings exclude Base. See Appendix H for the definition of the metrics.
Figure 17: Predictive standard deviation fields on Ahmed. The rows show in order: ground truth, base prediction, base error and the standard deviation of a sample for each of the UQ baselines.

Figure 18: Compute costs on all benchmarks: Train overhead (the additional time needed on top of a trained base operator to enable UQ), evaluation time, peak train memory, and peak evaluation memory on all benchmarks. REEF-GP maintains an excellent balance between training overhead and inference speed, while peak evaluation memory is higher.

Table 11: OOD robustness across MMD-based geometric quantiles. Test samples are partitioned into four groups by MMD distance from the training distribution: Q1 (closest, in-distribution) through Q4 (furthest, most geometrically novel). For each method we report rL2 (↓) and NLL (↓); rL2 in percent. Best in bold, second best underlined.
Figure 21: Predictive standard deviation fields on a test Car sample from Q3.
Figure 22: Predictive standard deviation fields on a test Car sample from Q4.
Figure 23: Predictive standard deviation fields on a test Ahmed sample from Q1.
